speaker: interviewer
text: --of Max Eisen. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here this farmer is giving me a lift on his horse and wagon back to my hometown and we have to travel about approximately 15 or so kilometers. I'm sitting in the back, in some hay there. And my thoughts are, you know, what I'm going to see in my town, you know. Hoping that perhaps a miracle will happen, and somebody will be there, perhaps somebody from my family. And the farmer didn't talk to me. And I was kind of afraid to talk to him. He really, I could see, was not interested, you know, in what happened. And I was perhaps thinking that maybe he is, he's not so happy to see me at all. And finally we arrived near my home. And he let me off. And my home was destroyed, because the front line came right through our town. And the roof was totally collapsed. And on one wall, there were some big holes, like a hole from rocket fire, from these-- from the Russian rockets. And so, I went in to a neighbor, and I told him who I was. And they couldn't believe me. And I could-- I noticed some credenza, you know. We used to have furniture, like there were credenzas in the kitchen, and beautiful wooden credenzas made from nice wood for our clothing. And I could see a few pieces of our furniture there. And I was looking at it. And they sort of noticed. And I was-- they were not happy to see me, altogether, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, I walked into town. And I was walking through-- by these houses where Jews lived. And there was nobody there. It was like, more or less, like walking in a cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in town, I found out that there was a Jewish man, this Bodner, that used to have a bicycle store. He came back with the partisans. He somehow got in with the partisans. And he was back in our town since January of '45. And he was living in one of the homes that belonged to a Jewish family. And there were a couple of people, survivors, that came back were living there, in this house. So it was sort of a shelter, you know, that I, yeah, I was able to put my head down and-- And then I found out that our neighbor, this Ili, this Christian woman, you know. She was living in town. She moved from her house, into this town of Moldova. And I went to visit her. And in fact, I stayed with her for about a week. She looked after me. She washed me, and she washed my clothes, and she got me some clothes. And I got very sick. I got pleurisy. I remember, I had terrible pains in my chest. I couldn't breathe. And they had to take me on a wagon back to-- about a week after I came home-- to a hospital in Kalsa. And I was in that hospital for a while. They had to drain all this liquid from my lungs. And I was on a dry diet, salt free diet, and all that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kept thinking, well, what is my future going to be here, you know? I had to-- I had nobody, no relatives that came back. And I, I was looking for some kind of family or some-- somewhere where I can feel some comfort from somebody. And there was nothing. And so I heard about a youth-- a Jewish youth group that was forming. And-- in the Czech part of the country, outside, about 100 kilometers from Prague, in a place called Marienbad. So I tracked down-- and you know, we could get on the train at that time, because we were displaced people, you know. We didn't have to pay any you know. People let us ride the trains, and so on. So I arrived in this place called Marienbad. And there was this-- it was an orphanage, actually. But it was also a place of learning. It was called a Yeshiva, where we were learning Hebrew. But those of us that were born in Czechoslovakia were allowed to go and learn some trades. There were many youngsters from Hungary, Romania, and Poland that came here. They were all my age-- 15, 16, 17, and 18-year-olds. So at least I had a-- the town gave us a pension. It was about a four-story, like a small hotel, a pension. And we each had, like two of us were in a, in a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a communal kitchen. We had a couple that was doing the cooking for us. And you know, life was great. It was a beautiful place. And I started to work as an apprentice for a dental technician-- for a dentist. And I worked in the lab, with a master mechanic. And he was a fantastic teacher, this mechanic, you know. He taught me how to do dental-- you know, how to be a good dental technician. And I was there for three years. And during these three years in Marienbad, I came-- I became a normal human being again, you know. With these friends, you know, we became a total family. That's the only family we all had. And these were three best years of my life, you know. We had fun. And you know, we had to get a lot out of our system. And Marienbad being a spa, and it's famous for its baths and so on. And I remember maharajahs coming from India, with 300 people, you know, who were looking after them, you know. They took over a whole hotel, you know. This was such a gorgeous place. And I was there till '48. And all of a sudden, out of the blue, the communists took over the Czech government, which was a democracy. And the communists took it over. And our life came tumbling down again, you know. It feel apart, everything fell apart. We had to run away from here. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have received some permits, from a Rabbi Price in Toronto, to come here as students. And he sent us these permits, prior to the communists taking over Czechoslovakia. And so we went to Prague, going-- getting through all the medical exams and so on. And had the communists not taken over, it would have been a smooth process, you know, of getting our medical and everything. And we would have been gone. But since the communists took over, we had to get a stamp from the Secret Service or the [CZECH], I think it was. The National Secret Service. And they would not allow us to come out. Because we were-- most of us are Czech-- most of the Slovaks, we were all of military age. And so we had to try and get some stamps put on these passports. We had to buy Hungarian passports, because I was coming here as a Hungarian to Canada, not as a Slovak. And while we were looking for somebody to do these papers for us, these false papers, we were raided in a hotel. And six of us were caught. At that time, under the communists, you could not stay in Prague in a hotel for more than one night. If you stayed more than one night, they had to report you to the police, because everybody had to be on a job. This was a-- now a communist state, you know. It was a police state. So-- and we were caught in this dragnet, you know. And we were taken to jail. I was in jail there for about six months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was a terrible experience. Because I thought that from here, I'll never get out. This is what they told me. And it was a real depressant, you know, after-- having an experience like this, after going through the concentration camp, and here I am sitting with political prisoners. I was in a room, perhaps six feet by 12. There were six of us in this room. Two triple bunks, and we had one small table and two benches, and there was a toilet. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned prison life, what it's all about. We were allowed to go outside for exercises for 15 minutes. The rest of the time, we had to sit in, in this cell. And after six months, a Jewish organization, they paid a ransom for us. I think they paid $2,000 for each of us. And they let us out of this jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which organization that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I think it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a Zionist organization. And I remember, on that day when on the loudspeaker, I heard my name called in the jail system, you know, this was a very big jail, strictly for political prisoners. And I heard my name and some others. Because these other six-- the six of us that we were together, we were totally separated. We never saw each other during this time. Because they knew that we were from one case, so we were separated in different wings of the jail. And we were taken down to the head of the jail, you know, the, the man in charge. And there was somebody who was there, whom I vaguely saw his face somewhere, in a-- at a restaurant in Prague, in a Jewish restaurant. And we had to sign a document guaranteeing that we're leaving the country in 24 hours. And I was glad to sign this, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this head of the jail told us that, look, if you don't leave the country, if we catch you again, you're in for life. So as soon as we got out of the jail, we asked this man, is it true? Are you taking us somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no, go away to Slovakia. You know, the jail was in Prague. And we got on the train. And we went to Slovakia. It's like, you know, Czechs and Slovaks, you know. The system was not working too close. So we thought that maybe in Slovakia, they won't know what happened to us in Prague. But the aim was to get out of the country. And so I heard about Hungarians Jews that were coming over to Slovakia, to Bratislava. And I went to Bratislava with my friend. And we got in with this Hungarian group. And one day, we were cleared, and we were taken over to Austria. And we arrived in the Russian zone in Austria. And again, this [NON-ENGLISH], they picked us up with Army trucks. And they took us to Vienna, into the American sector. Vienna, at that time, was in the middle of the Russian zone, divided into four sectors-- American, French, English, and Russian. So I arrived in the English sector of Vienna-- in the American sector of Vienna, at the Rothschild Hospital. This is where all the-- all of us displaced persons gathered, in this Rothschild Hospital. And so I started to make inquiries, whether there was a Canadian embassy in Vienna. And I found out that there wasn't. I was told that the only embassy is in Salzburg, in the American zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, I had a problem. I had to find a way of getting out of Vienna, across the Russian zone, into the American zone. I found a Czech organization who told me that they will take me across. It will cost me so many shillings. And I didn't have much money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was willing to do that, because that was my hope. That was my only hope to come to Canada. Because a couple of my friends were already here. And I was-- I had no family anywhere else that I knew of. So I had to. It was important that I get here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I paid. And they told me how we are going to leave. I had to walk into the French sector, to the railroad station. And we got on a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you'll have to watch this Austrian, who was wearing these lederhosen. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he gets off the train, you'll get off the train. And you'll have to spread yourself out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in, in the train, started out. And we went into the Russian sector. And I see that this man gets off the train. And I see somebody else got off, some of the other people, and about six of us-- he was taking six of us across.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I got off the train, I got on this platform, and I said, something is very familiar here. And I looked up on the railroad station on the name. And I saw Melk. And I just about died, because this is where I was in camp for a while, in Melk. And I thought this must be some kind of a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they're trying to lead us down a garden path here. But no, we walked through this little town and into the mountains. And this man said to me, said to us, looking down the trail, he said, you'll see a Russian guard coming by there, in about 10 minutes, soldiers on horses. And he said, when you see them, wait about five minutes. And then run like hell and jump across this ditch, and you are in the American sector. And you know, all this happened. And we ran. We jumped across this ditch into the mud and muck. And then we took a train in a town called [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we took this train to Linz. And I arrived in this displaced person camp, called Ebelsberg, which was outside of Linz. And here, I was-- the next thing I did was go to Salzburg, to the Canadian embassy. And I asked them to bring my permit from Prague to Salzburg, which they did. But they gave me a very difficult time. They did not want to give me a visa. Because they said that I had a spot on my lung. And I was sent for medical examinations and x-rays, about five times. And I couldn't understand why none of the Jews were allowed to come to Canada. But next door to us was a, another DP camp of Ukrainians, who were SS soldiers, you know, fighting with a Germans, you know. And all these guys were allowed to come to Canada and the United States and Australia. And we Jews were not allowed to come here. I understand why this happened, because of the restricted rules of Jewish immigration to Canada now. But so this was in '49. Late October, I finally got my permit to come to Canada from the Canadian Embassy. And I tell you, I was a real happy young man. And a couple days later, they got me on a train. And we went to Hamburg. I got on this boat, the Samaria. And we came in the hold of the boat as DPs. And I arrived in Quebec City, I think it was October the 29th of 1949. And when I saw-- when the boat entered a gulf of St. Lawrence, and I saw Quebec City, I saw the immense size of this country. And finally, we got off the boat. And I was really happy. It was a new start for me, you know. It was a new life. And I was given a cup of coffee and a sandwich. And they put us on a train. And I had $0.50 in my pocket. I couldn't buy any other food on the train, because I had no money. And I guess a couple of days later, I arrived in Toronto, at the Union Station. And one of my friends picked me up. And he took me to a place where I was going to live. It was on Huron Street, with a family, Mr. and Mrs. Kass, where three of us survivors, you know, three of us youngsters, we lived there with her. And we became a ward of the Jewish Family and Child Service, here in Toronto. And they helped us out. They paid for our room and board, until we could find a job. And since it was winter, they sent us to-- yes, to Tip Top Tailors. And they made a coat for us. We were given a winter coat and a suit. And soon enough-- soon after, I found a job. Or they found a job, they sent me to a job. And I was working at first in a luggage factory on Spadina here. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was working there. I think they were paying me $0.20 an hour. And finally, I got another job. It was a place where they were making wedding albums. And there was a lady, her name was Rose. And finally, she asked me every-- well, she saw every day I was bringing my sandwiches. And it was the same sandwich, a kaiser bun with cottage cheese and an egg. And she finally said, well, I'll exchange your lunch. And she eventually asked me to come up to her house. And that was really great. Because you know, I sort of made contact with her family. And that's where I met my future wife. Her daughter became my wife eventually. And you know, she helped me a great deal, you know my mother-in-law. She's passed away, now. And so I became a Canadian. And I went through-- I finally found myself a career. And I built a manufacturing company. We are a family business. And we have twin sons. And we have two granddaughters. And I'm a really happy person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you tell the story of what happened to you during the war, to people today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for the last four years I-- through the remembrance, the Holocaust Remembrance Committee here in Toronto, I've been going to schools and talking to students in Ontario and even in other provinces. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've been in touch with many kids, telling them the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of reactions do you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, in the main, I get very good reactions. In many places, kids have never known that there was a Holocaust, you know. And I was in some-- for instance, I was in Frederickton, New Brunswick, you know, which is sort of off on the east coast, you know. And there are not very many Jewish people living there. And I had a very good reception. Kids were very interested. And there was, in fact, you know, many asked me-- they didn't know what to do for me. They asked me, will I come for dinner, you know. They just wanted to do so much for me and you know, there was really, there really wasn't a time. Many times kids, they prepare all kinds of poems. I get so many letters from these kids, you know, after I've spoken to them, you know. I've collected all these things. And it's really rewarding. I feel that if I can leave something with a few kids, you know, that it may help, you know, to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] future generations, to not to falling into the same trap.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that it could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it could happen. I am-- this is only 50 years after liberation. And you know, we have all kinds of Nazi groups trying to indoctrinate children. And we have the internet. I just saw the other day on the Canadian news, what kind of information they can get on the internet. And this is quite a worry. And I think it could happen anywhere. It can happen right here in Canada. And I think our job is to try to tell the kids to be very careful, you know, what propaganda, what they hear, that not to buy, you know, anything they hear, you know, just readily. That they have to think about it and research it. Because these people have got powerful things-- tools that they supply. And they use the same idea that the Nazis used, you know. If you tell a lie, and it's big enough. You know, if you tell it three times, people will believe it. So, I think what we all have to do is teach these kids not to accept these-- this propaganda readily. And the future generations of Jews, what should they know about your life? What message would you like to leave them with? Well, future generations, I hope that they will be involved. Second and third generations must be involved with this. Because this is too important for any, especially our people, to-- that this cannot be forgotten. And we, as witnesses, we sort of have to-- we're handing this over to them. So in, in, in essence, they become witnesses themselves. And they have to fight, you know, bigotry and racism. They have to be in there, you know, and fighting this, this mentality, you know. And I think that's what they can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for giving us the opportunity to interview you. And now, we'll view some of the pictures you've saved. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you. From left to right. This picture was taken in 1940. It's an official picture that we had to take, every Jewish family had to take, for official purposes. On the back, there is a stamp. And an official of the township is certifying that this is a family of Zoltan Eisen. And I'll go from left to right. It's my youngest brother, Alfred, or his Hebrew name was Moishe. My mother Ethel. And myself in the middle. And my father, Zoltan. And on the right, on the far right, my brother Eugene, or [NON-ENGLISH]. That was his Hebrew name. This shows the wedding. It's a wedding picture of my uncle, his name was Jeno, and his wife Irene, in 1933. And the wedding was, I believe, in our town of Moldava. This is a picture of my father and my uncle and a cousin. His name was Dhezor Kessner. And they were in a forced labor camp. There are just soldiers and the man in charge of this unit. And this picture was taken in [NON-ENGLISH], which is in the southern part of Hungary, which was, prior to that, it was Yugoslavia. This is where they were in a forced labor camp. This is a picture from the forced labor camp in [NON-ENGLISH]. I guess this was on a day off. On the left side is my father and our cousin Dhezor Kessner, and my uncle, Jeno Eisen. This picture shows the yeshiva where we were in Marienbad. This was taken in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of the boys have already gone to Ireland. And the Rabbi in the middle, his name Rabbi Stern. And he came from Budapest. And we are just the remaining group. There are about 20 of, of us, already have gone to Ireland, prior to this picture being taken. And most of these boys are here in Canada, some in the states, and some in Australia. This picture is also taken in Marienbad, Czechoslovakia in 1947. That's me on the left side. I was smoking a pipe. And then my friend, Sam [NON-ENGLISH], he's here in Toronto. And that little guy, he was being trained in the Czech Air Force. And he went to Israel to fight in the Israeli Air Force in 1948. So this was taken in 1947. This is a picture of my house, where I was born. This was taken in 1950. This house was destroyed during the war. And the government rebuilt it. And this lady that you can see, with a little girl standing there, she was our neighbor across the street. Her name was Ili Klinka. She sent me this picture. And the house is an L-shape house. So you only see the front part-- the lower part of the L. It goes a long way in the back. To the right, there was a big yard, with a lumber yard on the right side. And in the back, there were a couple acres of-- there was a fruit orchard. And we had a-- there was a fence and a big wooden gate in the front. And this is all gone. It's not on this picture here, of course. This is a picture of our identical twin sons, Ed and Larry. And this is their bar mitzvah picture. It was in taken in January-- January the 8th, 1968.

speaker: interviewee
text: '66, pardon me. This is a picture of our son Larry. He's the father of two girls. Julie on the left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Julie was 11 on this picture. And Amy on the right, and she's 13 years old. This is a picture of our son Ed. And he's the uncle of Amy, on the right. This picture, my wife Ivy, myself, Amy on the right, and Julie sitting in the front. And this was taken in '94.
